content.text: Accessing Account Settings

AWS account settings are spread across a few locations in the console. The most important settings live under the account dropdown menu (click the account name or email address in the top-right of the console header) and in the dedicated
Account
page that it links to.

The account dropdown menu reveals: your current sign-in identity (email for root, username for IAM user, or role ARN for assumed roles), your account ID (12-digit number), and links to Account, Organization, Service Quotas, Billing Dashboard, Switch Role, and Settings. Understanding each of these entries helps you navigate account management efficiently.
The Account Settings Page

attrs.alt: AWS Account Settings page structure showing account information, contact info, regions, billing IAM access, security challenges, and tax settings sections

content.text: Clicking
Account
in the dropdown takes you to the Account Settings page. This page is accessible only to the root user and to IAM users/roles that have been explicitly granted
aws-portal:ViewAccount
or billing-related permissions. It contains six major sections:
Account Information
The Account Information section shows your account's immutable and configurable identifiers:
Account ID
The 12-digit account ID (e.g.,
) is the primary identifier for your AWS account. It is immutable — you cannot change it. The account ID appears in:
ARNs (Amazon Resource Names): arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket vs arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-role
Cross-account trust policies (the principal's account ID is listed explicitly)
CloudTrail logs (every API call records the caller's account ID)
The sign-in URL when you use an account alias
AWS Support tickets and case correspondence
Account Alias
An account alias is a human-readable name you assign to replace the account ID in the IAM sign-in URL. Instead of:

# Default IAM sign-in URL (uses account ID)
https://123456789012.signin.aws.amazon.com/console

# With account alias set to "mycompany-prod"
https://mycompany-prod.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
Account aliases must be globally unique across all AWS accounts. Setting one does not affect root account sign-in (root always uses email + password). It only affects the IAM user sign-in URL.
You can set the alias via CLI or the console:

# Set account alias
aws iam create-account-alias --account-alias mycompany-prod

# Get current alias
aws iam list-account-aliases

# Delete alias
aws iam delete-account-alias --account-alias mycompany-prod
Canonical User ID
The Canonical User ID is a long alphanumeric string used in S3 bucket ACLs and CloudFront signed URLs to grant cross-account access without exposing the account ID. It looks like:

# Example Canonical User ID
79a59df900b949e55d96a1e698fbacedfd6e09d98eacf8f8d5218e7cd47ef2be

You rarely need the Canonical User ID directly — modern S3 access control uses bucket policies and IAM roles instead of ACL grants by canonical user. However, if you inherit an older AWS setup with cross-account S3 ACLs, this is where to find the ID.
Contact Information
The Contact Information section stores the account's registered name, company, address, phone number, and email. AWS uses this information for:
Billing correspondence (invoices, payment failure notices)
Security notifications (e.g., compromised credentials alerts)
Account recovery verification (phone call or email confirmation)
Tax invoices (some countries require a legal entity name and address on invoices)
Alternate Contacts
AWS supports three types of alternate contacts, each pointing to a different person or team email/phone:
Alternate Contact Type
Receives notifications for
Billing
Invoice emails, payment failures, cost anomaly alerts, budget threshold alerts
Operations
Service health events, maintenance notifications, EC2 scheduled maintenance
Security
Security vulnerability notices, compromised credential alerts, abuse reports

Setting alternate contacts is a critical early account configuration step. Without them, all notifications go to the root account email — which is often a shared inbox or an email that engineers rarely monitor. Set the Billing contact to your finance team, Operations to your on-call team or PagerDuty forwarding address, and Security to your security team.

# Set alternate contact via CLI
aws account put-alternate-contact   --alternate-contact-type SECURITY   --email-address security-team@example.com   --name "Security Team"   --phone-number "+1-555-0100"   --title "AWS Security Notifications"   --region us-east-1

# Other types: BILLING, OPERATIONS
# Note: account API only available in us-east-1
IAM User and Role Access to Billing
By default, only the root user can view billing pages in the AWS console, even if an IAM user has
billing:*
permissions in their IAM policy. This is a deliberate AWS security choice: billing visibility requires an explicit opt-in from the root user.
To grant IAM users and roles access to billing:
Sign in as root.
Go to Account Settings → IAM User and Role Access to Billing Information.
Click Edit → select "Activate IAM Access" → Update.
After activating, IAM policies that grant billing permissions (like
aws-portal:ViewBilling
or
billing:GetBillingData
) take effect. Without this activation, those policies have no effect — IAM users see an access denied error on billing pages regardless of their policy.
Important:

This setting must be configured once per account and is not inherited by member accounts in an AWS Organization. Each member account needs its own activation if you want IAM users in those accounts to view their billing.
Recommended Billing IAM Policy
This policy grants read-only access to billing and cost data. Attach it to a billing-viewers IAM group or role rather than individual users.
Region Settings and Opt-In Regions
The Account Settings page lists all AWS regions with their opt-in status. Regions fall into two categories:
Always-enabled: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1, and most other commercial regions — active for every account automatically
Opt-in required: newer regions that must be explicitly enabled (ap-east-1 Hong Kong, me-south-1 Bahrain, af-south-1 Cape Town, etc.)

To enable an opt-in region from Account Settings → Regions → click Enable on the target region. Enabling takes a few minutes and makes the region appear in the console's region selector. Disabling a region you previously enabled prevents new resources from being created there but does not affect existing resources.

# Enable a region via CLI
aws account enable-region --region-name ap-east-1

# Check region status
aws account list-regions   --region-opt-status-contains ENABLED ENABLED_BY_DEFAULT   --output table
Security Challenges

The Security Challenges section lets you set a security question and answer for your account. AWS Support uses this as an identity verification step when you call for account recovery — for example, if you lose access to the root account's email and MFA device simultaneously.
Unlike MFA, which proves you possess a device, the security challenge proves you have knowledge. It is a last-resort identity verification:
Choose a question whose answer is stable and not publicly discoverable (avoid 'What city were you born in?' if your LinkedIn profile shows it)
Record the answer in a secure location (password manager or sealed envelope) — you may need it years later
The question and answer are never displayed after setting; you can only reset them
Note:

AWS is moving toward relying more on MFA and registered contact information for account recovery, and less on knowledge-based answers. The security challenge remains available but is supplementary to MFA configuration.
Tax Settings
The Tax Settings section (also accessible from the Billing console) stores VAT/GST registration numbers and business registration information. AWS uses this to determine how to tax your invoices:
EU businesses registered for VAT: enter your VAT ID so AWS invoices apply reverse charge (B2B VAT treatment) rather than adding VAT to your bill
India businesses with GST registration: enter your GSTIN for proper GST treatment on invoices
US tax-exempt organizations: upload a tax exemption certificate
Other countries: consult the Tax Settings page for country-specific fields

Tax settings affect future invoices only — they cannot be retroactively applied to past charges. Set these up immediately when creating an account that will incur taxable AWS charges in a VAT/GST jurisdiction.
Console Settings (Unified Settings)
Clicking
Settings
in the account dropdown takes you to the Console Unified Settings page — separate from the Account Settings page. Unified Settings controls your console user experience:
Setting
Options / Effect
Language
English, French, German, Japanese, Korean, Portuguese (Brazil), Spanish, Chinese (Simplified/Traditional), Italian
Default region
Set the region the console opens to when you visit a regional service
Recently visited
Clear the Recently Visited history in Console Home
Favorites
View and manage pinned Favorites from one place
Cookie preferences
Control analytics and functional cookie consent

Console settings are stored per-browser in browser local storage, not server-side per account. Changing them on one machine or browser does not affect other machines or incognito windows. If you clear browser data, settings reset to defaults.
Switch Role — Cross-Account Access
The
Switch Role

option in the account dropdown lets you assume an IAM role in another AWS account without signing out and signing back in. This is the primary mechanism for engineers to work across multiple accounts in an organization.
Switching to a Role
Click account dropdown → Switch Role.
Enter the target Account ID (12-digit number).
Enter the Role Name (must match an IAM role in the target account that trusts your source account).
Optionally set a Display Name and Color — the console header changes to show a custom color bar so you know you are in a different account.
Click Switch Role. The console reloads in the context of the assumed role.

While in a switched role, the account dropdown shows the role name and a 'Back to' link. Clicking it returns to your original account. Sessions last up to the role's maximum session duration (default 1 hour, up to 12 hours).
Setting Up a Switchable Role
For Switch Role to work, the target account must have an IAM role with a trust policy allowing your account to assume it:
This trust policy allows any IAM principal from account
to assume the role, provided they authenticated with MFA. The MFA condition prevents automated processes (which don't have MFA devices) from assuming the role accidentally.
Service Quotas
The Service Quotas service (accessible from the account dropdown) shows default limits for every AWS service resource type and lets you request increases. Examples of default quotas:
EC2: 32 vCPUs on-demand (standard instance families) — limits new accounts to small workloads initially
Lambda: 1,000 concurrent executions per region
S3: 100 buckets per account
VPC: 5 VPCs per region
RDS: 40 DB instances per region
Quota increases are account-specific and region-specific. A quota increase in us-east-1 does not automatically apply to eu-west-1. Request increases in each region where you need higher limits:

# Request a Service Quota increase via CLI
aws service-quotas request-service-quota-increase   --service-code ec2   --quota-code L-1216C47A   --desired-value 192   --region us-east-1

# List current quotas for EC2
aws service-quotas list-service-quotas   --service-code ec2   --region us-east-1   --output table

# Get quota status
aws service-quotas get-requested-service-quota-change   --request-id <request-id>   --region us-east-1
AWS automatically approves most quota increase requests within minutes for moderate increases. Large increases (e.g., 1,000+ vCPUs) may require a business justification and take 1–3 business days.
AWS Organizations and Account Settings

When your account belongs to an AWS Organization, some account settings are controlled at the management account level. Understanding which settings are organization-wide versus account-specific is essential when troubleshooting access or compliance issues.
Management account

is the root account that created the organization. It has full control over member accounts through Service Control Policies (SCPs) and consolidated billing. Member accounts can still manage their own settings, but SCPs can restrict what actions are allowed.
Consolidated Billing

content: : The management account pays for all member accounts. Individual account billing settings (tax info, payment methods) are overridden by the management account's settings.

content.text: Service Control Policies (SCPs)

content: : Can block access to account settings such as region opt-in or IAM billing access changes in member accounts.

content.text: Account Alias

content: : Each account sets its own alias independently — not inherited from the management account.

content.text: Alternate Contacts

content: : Can be set per-member account or overridden via organization policies in some configurations.

content.text: To check if your account is part of an organization and identify the management account:

# Check organization membership
aws organizations describe-organization

# Output shows:
# MasterAccountId: the management account
# MasterAccountEmail: management account email
# Id: organization ID (o-xxxx)

# List your account's organizational units (OUs)
aws organizations list-parents --child-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text)

Member accounts cannot leave an organization freely — the management account must remove them, or the member account owner must explicitly initiate removal (which also requires management account approval in some configurations).
Programmatic Account Management with the AWS CLI

Many account settings that live in the console can also be read and modified programmatically using the AWS CLI. This is especially useful when auditing multiple accounts at scale or automating baseline configurations.
Reading and Setting Account Alias
The account alias is often the first customization made after creating a new account. It makes the console sign-in URL memorable and improves audit log readability.

# Get current account alias(es)
aws iam list-account-aliases

# Create an alias (must be globally unique, 3-63 chars, lowercase alphanumeric + hyphens)
aws iam create-account-alias --account-alias my-company-prod

# Remove an alias
aws iam delete-account-alias --account-alias my-company-prod

# The sign-in URL becomes:
# https://my-company-prod.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
Managing Alternate Contacts via CLI
Alternate contacts are critical for ensuring billing, operations, and security notifications reach the right teams. Verify them on every new account:

# Retrieve current alternate contacts (requires account:GetAlternateContact permission)
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type BILLING
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type OPERATIONS
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type SECURITY

# Set an alternate contact
aws account put-alternate-contact   --alternate-contact-type SECURITY   --email-address security-team@example.com   --name "Security Team"   --phone-number "+1-555-000-0000"   --title "Cloud Security"

# Delete an alternate contact (returns to root account owner)
aws account delete-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type SECURITY
The aws account CLI commands require the aws-account service endpoints. Ensure you are NOT specifying a region (these are global settings), or use --region us-east-1 if required.
Checking and Setting Region Opt-In

# List all regions and their opt-in status
aws account list-regions --output table

# Opt in to a region (e.g., ap-east-1 Hong Kong)
aws account enable-region --region-name ap-east-1

# Opt out of a region (existing resources still exist until you delete them)
aws account disable-region --region-name ap-east-1

# Check a specific region's status
aws account get-region-opt-status --region-name ap-east-1
Account Security Best Practices

The account settings page exposes several controls that, when properly configured, significantly reduce the risk of unauthorized access or data exposure. Work through this checklist when hardening a new account:
Root Account Hardening
MFA on root

content: : Enable hardware MFA or virtual MFA immediately. Root credentials bypass all IAM policies.

content.text: Delete root access keys

content: : Root should never have programmatic access keys. Check via:

content.text: aws iam get-account-summary | grep RootAccessKey
Root email address

content: : Use a group mailbox (e.g., aws-root@company.com), not a personal email. Update in Account Information.

content.text: Security challenge questions

content: : Set 3 security challenges as a recovery fallback for account support cases.

content.text: Billing and Cost Protection
Enable billing alerts

content: : In Billing Preferences, enable PDF invoices, free-tier alerts, and billing alerts (required before CloudWatch billing alarms work).

content.text: Set a budget

content: : AWS Budgets can send SNS/email alerts when spending exceeds a threshold.

content.text: IAM billing access

content: : Enable this setting to let your finance team use IAM roles instead of logging in as root.

content.text: Contact Information Accuracy

AWS uses account contact information to communicate about outages, compliance obligations, and account changes. Inaccurate information can result in missed critical notifications. Audit contact information quarterly:
Primary email: shared mailbox monitored by multiple team members
Phone number: a number answered 24/7, such as an on-call rotation number
Address: legal billing address matching what tax authorities expect
Alternate contacts: current team members with relevant responsibilities
Account Settings in Multi-Account Architectures

Large organizations use AWS Control Tower or manually structured AWS Organizations to manage dozens or hundreds of accounts. In these environments, account settings become infrastructure — managed as code and audited regularly.

AWS Control Tower can enforce baseline account configurations using Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) or Account Customizations. When a new account is vended, AFT runs customization pipelines that can:
Set account alias to match the account's purpose (e.g., prod-payments, dev-data-science)
Configure alternate contacts from a central source-of-truth
Opt in to required regions and opt out of forbidden ones
Apply SCPs from the organization management account
Enable AWS Config and Security Hub in every account automatically

# Example: boto3 script to audit alternate contacts across all org accounts
import boto3

org_client = boto3.client('organizations')
paginator = org_client.get_paginator('list_accounts')

for page in paginator.paginate():
    for account in page['Accounts']:
        account_id = account['Id']
        # Assume a cross-account role in each member account
        sts = boto3.client('sts')
        assumed = sts.assume_role(
            RoleArn=f'arn:aws:iam::{account_id}:role/OrgAuditRole',
            RoleSessionName='AuditSession'
        )
        creds = assumed['Credentials']
        acc_client = boto3.client('account',
            aws_access_key_id=creds['AccessKeyId'],
            aws_secret_access_key=creds['SecretAccessKey'],
            aws_session_token=creds['SessionToken'],
            region_name='us-east-1'
        )
        try:
            sec = acc_client.get_alternate_contact(AlternateContactType='SECURITY')
            print(f"{account_id}: SECURITY contact = {sec['AlternateContact']['EmailAddress']}")
        except acc_client.exceptions.ResourceNotFoundException:
            print(f"{account_id}: NO SECURITY contact set — ACTION REQUIRED")

The OrgAuditRole must exist in every member account and must trust the management account (or the audit account) in its trust policy. This role pattern is the foundation of centralized cloud governance.
Summary

AWS Account Settings covers account identification (account ID, alias, canonical user ID), contact information with separate Billing/Operations/Security alternate contacts, the IAM billing access toggle (must be enabled for IAM users to see billing pages), opt-in region activation, security challenge configuration, tax registration, console user experience settings, and the Switch Role mechanism for cross-account access. The Service Quotas page shows and manages per-service resource limits. Most of these settings are configured once during account setup and rarely changed, but understanding where they live is essential when troubleshooting billing access issues, account recovery scenarios, or setting up cross-account workflows.
